topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt abrasive against my eyes, a harsh interrogation I didn't have the energy to answer. I stood near the velvet rope, my hands tucked deep into the pockets of my worn jeans, feeling the familiar, heavy drag in my limbs. The canvases—my canvases—were hung in a blinding, indifferent spotlight. A woman, sharp-edged and radiating an almost aggressive confidence, was gesturing toward a particularly vibrant piece, her smile too wide, too practiced. I watched the way the light caught the texture of my pigment, a strange, detached observation. A dull ache settled behind my sternum, a deep, pervasive tiredness that made even the simple act of breathing feel like an effort. I wanted to retreat, to dissolve into the cool, shadowed corner near the exit, but my feet remained rooted to the polished floor.

[story 2]

I traced the grain of the mahogany railing, my gaze fixed on the ceiling where dust motes danced in the shafts of afternoon sun. Everything seemed muted, the colors of the room washed out, mirroring the state of my own internal landscape. Then I saw them: my sketches, displayed like trophies on pedestals. A sudden, sharp clench tightened my jaw. Someone was talking animatedly near the central display, their laughter echoing too loudly in the quiet space. My shoulders slumped, a heavy surrender to the moment. I felt a strange numbness spread through my fingers, making it hard to grip anything, even the air. I took a slow, shallow breath, tasting the stale air, and decided, quite simply, that moving forward felt like climbing a sheer cliff face.

[story 3]

The silence in the room was oppressive, thick enough to choke on. I leaned against a cold marble pillar, my gaze drifting over the array of my creations. They were there, undeniable, yet utterly alien in this setting. A faint tremor ran through my hands, a physical manifestation of the inertia pulling me down. I watched the patrons—their focused attention, their hushed appraisals—and felt utterly disconnected, as if observing a distant, slow-motion film. "It's quite striking," a voice remarked nearby, the tone dripping with proprietary satisfaction. I didn't look up. My eyelids felt weighted with lead. I just wanted the floor to swallow me whole, to stop the constant, draining awareness of being observed.
